title: ‘When I get home, my whole body aches’: extreme heat saps health and wages of India’s poorest workers - The Guardian
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Delhi
full_text:
‘When I get home, my whole body aches’: extreme heat saps health and wages of India’s poorest workers
Exclusive: Outdoor workers losing up to 9% of income as weather becomes ‘poverty multiplier’, research says
Extreme heat is pushing some of India’s poorest workers further into poverty, with income losses of up to 9% for outdoor workers, new research has found.
Analysis done by the International Institute for Environment and Development (IIED), and shared exclusively with the Guardian, found that extreme heat is acting as a “poverty multiplier” for hundreds of millions of workers in India’s informal sector.
Researchers found that those working in highly physical jobs in sectors such as agriculture, construction and transport lost on average 6.6% of their salaries in 2024 due to extreme heat. Outdoor workers suffered the most, losing close to 9% of their salary in 2024, amounting to £112 ($151).
The economic loss was the highest in the arid desert region of Rajasthan, an area with prolonged heatwaves and a rising death toll due to extreme heat. Informal workers in the region lost equivalent to 8.3% of their income, due to the gruelling effects of extreme heat on the body making them unable to work.
The IIED research estimated that each year, 21.1 working days were lost in India due to extreme heat, an unaffordable loss for many of the 550 million workers in the informal sector.
Ritu Bharadwaj, climate finance director at the IIED, said the research showed how extreme heat was deepening poverty and worsening existing structural inequalities in India, which is aiming to be a developed nation in the next two decades.
She said: “Heat is quietly pushing these families deeper into poverty.
“As the heat rises, their efficiency goes down, so they earn less. With lower income their food intake falls. And because they cannot afford to stop, they keep working through it, doing lasting damage to their health. They are paying for a crisis they did nothing to cause, with their health and with their savings.”
Human-caused climate breakdown is making every heatwave in the world more intense and more likely to happen. India, a country where nine out of 10 work in the informal economy, could become one of the first places where heatwaves break the human survivability limit, a World Bank report said.
The research estimates that heat could cost the Indian economy at least £58bn – equivalent to 2% of its GDP – in a combination of wages lost due to reduced productivity, worksite closures and long-term losses from heat-related illnesses.
The analysis accounts for three ways heat exposure reduces hours worked: heat-related illnesses, a reduction in the capacity of work or maintaining normal productivity, and time lost when workplaces suspend their operations.
Informal workers include some of the most socially and economically marginalised people, who lack employment security, obliging them to work through illness and tolerate unsafe conditions.
Access to drinking water, shade, rest areas, sanitation and medical support at the worksite was poor, said the report. Many workers then returned to homes that provided “no relief from heat”.
Eight construction workers who spoke to the Guardian all said they had repeatedly fallen ill over the past four months because of the heat, forcing them to miss work and lose income. Some had collapsed and sustained serious injuries but were given no compensation by employers. Some were even refused pay.
Inder and Laxmi Kumar, a husband and wife toiling on a dusty construction site in Gurugram, a satellite city outside Delhi, said they had both been confined to bed with fever, body aches and exhaustion. But after losing eight days’ wages, they could no longer afford to stay at home.
“I felt like I would collapse, like my body was on fire,” Laxmi said. “We kept working because if we stopped, we wouldn’t be paid.”
Doctors told the couple they were suffering from dehydration and heat exhaustion and advised them to rest.
The couple earn 800 rupees (£6) each a day, supporting their two young sons and four other family members back in their home village in Madhya Pradesh. They start around 9am and spend eight to 10 hours a day mixing cement, carrying construction materials and laying bricks, with hardly any breaks and little shade beyond the unfinished structure itself. Their five-year-old son plays in the rubble.
In the past three months alone, they have fallen ill three times, losing nearly four weeks of work and spending much of their income on medicines.
“We had no choice but to come back,” Inder said. “Whatever we manage to save is spent the next time we fall sick. We hardly have anything left to send home. I just pray this heat ends. It’s torture.”
Inder said he did not know a single labourer at the site who had escaped illness this summer. The extreme heat often spoiled the food they made for lunch, making them sicker.
The family’s home is a 2.5 metre (8ft) square of tarpaulin shelter inside the unfinished building. In the evening, a fan stops when the diesel generators are switched off just an hour after they return from work, leaving them to sleep in the stifling heat.
“When I get home, my whole body aches,” said Laxmi. “But my son becomes restless in the heat and keeps crying. Then I still have to cook, and the room gets even hotter.
“It feels like we work and sleep inside an oven.”
For female labourers, the burden is often heavier. Many spend their working day also caring for young children.
Beside a water tanker on a construction site, 35-year-old Kumkumari poured water over her two-year-old daughter. “This is the only way to cool her down,” Kumkumari said. “She falls sick often, and when that happens I have to look after her instead of working.”
Prolonged exposure to heat and dehydration over time increases the risk of developing chronic kidney disease. The IIED research estimated that over years of repeated exposure to heat, one in 20 informal workers in India could develop chronic kidney disease – equivalent to more than 26 million workers.
Among them was 38-year-old Sattavir, who stood on the roadside in Gurugram, hoping for a contractor to offer him a day of labour work.
In the past two months, he could only work for eight days after he developed a kidney infection, brought on by working in extreme heat and repeated dehydration.
“The heat is killer,” he said. “In the last two months, I earned barely 7,000 rupees but spent around 9,000 rupees on medicines and treatment. I’m already in debt, but I’m still looking for work even though doctors say I need treatment for my kidneys.”
After waiting for several hours in the sun, Sattavir was not picked for work – the third consecutive day he returned home empty-handed. “When people see you’re weak, they don’t hire you,” he said.

title: Fresh spell of rain brings down heat in Delhi; IMD issues yellow alert - DD India
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Delhi
full_text:
Moderate rainfall lashed several parts of Delhi on Monday morning, bringing relief from the hot and humid conditions that had prevailed in the national capital over the past several days.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast more rainfall over the next two days and issued a yellow alert for the city, warning of light to moderate rain accompanied by thunderstorms at isolated places.
According to the IMD, Delhi’s maximum temperature is expected to remain between 32 degrees Celsius and 35 degrees Celsius over the next two days, while the minimum temperature is likely to range between 26 degrees Celsius and 28 degrees Celsius.
On Sunday, the city recorded a maximum temperature of 38.3 degrees Celsius, three degrees above the seasonal average, while the minimum temperature settled at 31 degrees Celsius, around four degrees above normal, reflecting the prevailing hot and humid conditions before the latest spell of rain.
The weather department said changing monsoon conditions over northern India are likely to bring intermittent cloud cover and scattered showers in the coming days, providing further respite from the heat.
The IMD also said the southwest monsoon is poised for a significant revival after a prolonged lull. The renewed activity is expected to begin over Northeast India before gradually extending to eastern, central and northern parts of the country during the early part of the week.
Meteorologists have forecast widespread rainfall accompanied by thunderstorms across several regions, with heavy rainfall likely at isolated places. The fresh spell is expected to bring relief to the Gangetic plains and parts of central India, which have experienced prolonged hot and humid weather in recent weeks.
The country has been witnessing a “break monsoon” phase, during which the monsoon trough shifts northwards, leading to a significant reduction in rainfall over central India and adjoining plains.
According to IMD data, India has so far received 244.6 mm of rainfall against the normal average of 323.1 mm, resulting in an overall rainfall deficit of 24 per cent.
(With IANS inputs)

title: Delhi CM Rekha Gupta issues guidelines to ensure public safety during heatwave - News On AIR
full_text:
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast heat wave conditions at isolated places in the national capital for tomorrow. According to the IMD, the maximum temperature in Delhi is expected to reach 42 degrees Celsius.
In view of the prevailing severe heatwave, Delhi Chief Minister Rekha Gupta has said that detailed guidelines have been issued to all departments to ensure public safety. Under directions issued by the Labour Ministry, employers have been asked to provide adequate rest to workers between 1 PM and 4 PM, along with arrangements for drinking water, shade, and other essential facilities. The Chief Minister said that schools have been instructed to remain fully equipped to safeguard children. This includes ensuring the availability of drinking water, proper ventilation, fans, and suitable clothing conditions. She added that students should be given ORS solutions before leaving school to prevent dehydration. The Chief Minister also informed that the Transport Department has been directed to set up water counters near bus stops across the city. These counters will provide cold drinking water and ORS solutions to commuters. She added that special measures are being taken in schools to safeguard children, including ensuring the availability of clean drinking water, fans, and appropriate clothing.
Meanwhile, on the healthcare front, Chief Minister Gupta assured that hospitals in the city are fully prepared to handle any emergency arising from the heatwave. She said all necessary medicines, facilities, and arrangements are in place. The Chief Minister also noted that water resources across the city will remain fully functional to meet public needs during the ongoing heatwave.

title: दिल्ली-एनसीआर में झमाझम बारिश से बदला मौसम, उमस भरी गर्मी से मिली राहत - Jagran
search_term: लू ("Central Delhi" OR "East Delhi" OR "New Delhi" OR "North Delhi" OR "North East Delhi" OR "North West Delhi" OR Shahdara OR "South Delhi" OR "South East Delhi" OR "South West Delhi" OR "West Delhi") Delhi
full_text:
दिल्ली-एनसीआर में झमाझम बारिश से बदला मौसम, उमस भरी गर्मी से मिली राहत
दिल्ली-एनसीआर में सोमवार सुबह से झमाझम बारिश हुई, जिससे मौसम सुहावना हो गया। इस बारिश ने उमस भरी गर्मी से लोगों को काफी राहत प्रदान की है। ...और पढ़ें
HighLights
दिल्ली-एनसीआर में सोमवार सुबह से मौसम का मिजाज बदला।
नोएडा, गुरुग्राम, गाजियाबाद में झमाझम बारिश दर्ज की गई।
बारिश से उमस भरी गर्मी से लोगों को राहत मिली।
डिजिटल डेस्क, नई दिल्ली। दिल्ली-एनसीआर में सोमवार सुबह से मौसम का मिजाज बदल गया है। एनसीआर के कई क्षेत्रों (नोएडा, गुरुग्राम, गाजियाबाद) में झमाझम बारिश से मौसम सुहावना हो गया है और उमस भरी गर्मी से कुछ राहत मिली है। बारिश से सुबह स्कूल और दफ्तर जाने वालों को परेशानी का सामना करना पड़ा।
न्यूनतम तापमान 26.8 डिग्री दर्ज
मौसम विभाग का पूर्वानुमान है कि अभी दो तीन बादलों की आवाजाही बनी रहेगी, हल्की से मध्यम स्तर की वर्षा भी संभावित है। अधिकतम तापमान 40 डिग्री से नीचे ही रहेगा। सोमवार को दिल्ली का न्यूनतम तापमान 26.8 डिग्री दर्ज किया गया। दिन का तापमान 33 डिग्री सेल्सियस के करीब रह सकता है।
दिन में बादलों की आवाजाही बनी रहेगी। बीच बीच मे धूप खिले रहने पर उमस से भी परेशानी होगी। उधर, सोमवार को सुबह साढ़े 9 बजे दिल्ली का एक्यूआई 134 दर्ज किया गया। इसे 'मध्यम' श्रेणी में रखा जाता है। एनसीआर के शहरों में भी हवा संतोषजनक से मध्यम श्रेणी में ही चल रही है। हाल फिलहाल इसमें खास बदलाव नहीं होगा।
बीते तीन दिन से आसमान में मंडराते बादलों की छाया में चिलचिलाती धूप से कुछ राहत मिली हुई है। रविवार को सुबह से शाम तक आसमान में घने बादल छाए रहे। बीच बीच में कुछ देर के लिए ही धूप निकली। हालांकि उमस भरी गर्मी बढ़ जाने की वजह से लोगों को काफी ज्यादा परेशानी रही।
खबरें और भी
मौसम विभाग का अनुमान है कि अगले दो दिन आसमान में घने बादल छाए रहेंगे। बारिश होने के भी आसार हैं। जिससे तापमान में गिरावट के साथ गर्मी से भी राहत रहेगी। बीते तीन दिन से लगातार आसमान में घने बादल छाए हुए हैं। लोगों को वर्षा होने की उम्मीद थी, लेकिन इंतजार रविवार को भी परा नहीं हो सका।
साइबर सिटी में बदला मौसम का मिजाज, बूंदाबांदी शुरू।
सुबह सवेरे से ही छाए घने बादलों को देखकर लगातार वर्षा होने के आसार लग रहे थे। बीच बीच में कुछ देर के लिए ही धूप निकली रही। पूरे दिन बादल छाए रहे, लेकिन वर्षा नहीं हुई। जिससे धूप से तो निजात मिली, लेकिन उमस भरी गर्मी से परेशानी काफी ज्यादा बढ़ गई।
